words: SAM SCHRAGER:  I first wanted to ask you about the name of the town you grew
up in and where it was located in Norway.
ED RAMSDALE: Well I grew up a place they called. Well, you mean the real
Well that was Ramsdale. That's the name of that. And then the nearest
shopping place where they had the store and a church and a school and where
the steamers used to come in tne ocean they called
And it was about seven English miles I think from Ramsdale down to
Ramsdale was the last place in that valley. There was a valley went on through
there and it went in two lakes. And the place where I came from was the last
place right up again the mountain. There was a mountain right
around like that.

speaker: SS
words: Your last name is Ramsdale and that's the name of the place. Is that how it

speaker: SS
words: worked?

speaker: ER
words: Yes. that's how it worked. I might tell you about my dad. He came from a

speaker: ER
words: place they called  And his name was  But when he married

speaker: ER
words: my mother, my mother was an only child^ and she inherited the farm there,

speaker: ER
words: you know, and then Dad, he changed his name from to Ramsdale. So

speaker: ER
words: still have that name of the neighborhood there. There was about four

speaker: ER
words: there in that neighborhood with that same name.

speaker: SS
words: That's how it works. Each neighborhood has it's own name and the people

speaker: SS
words: who live there have that name?

speaker: ER
words: Well, that is the way thev had it in the old days. And now my bother

speaker: ER
words: there, inherited the homeplace there and he had all girls. He didnt

speaker: ER
words: have any boys. So one of his daughter's got the homeplace that I growed up

speaker: ER
words: And she married afellah by the name of on.

speaker: ER
words: his name to Ramsdale when he married her.(Laughs) There's still Ramsdale

speaker: ER
words: there.

speaker: SS
words: I guess that's how much more important the land is there than here because

speaker: SS
words: to have your name go by the land

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, they really think a lot of their home farms—their homeplaces over

speaker: ER
words: there. It don't hardly ever go out of the family, very seldom. They try to

speaker: ER
words: keep it in the family. Some of 'era don't operate the land but still they

speaker: ER
words: any

speaker: ER
words: keep it, just to keep it in the family. And it's hard to buy land over there.

speaker: ER
words: There isn't hardly anthing for sale. And you never see any ads in the newspapers

speaker: ER
words: about land for sale or anything, very seldom. And last summer I was over

speaker: ER
words: there and the neighbor there, he kinda wanted to sell his place. He didn't

speaker: ER
words: have any kids or anything; he was gettin old. So I thought, Well, I'd like

speaker: ER
words: to buy that to the old homeplace and make it abigger place.' But

speaker: ER
words: they have a law there you gotta be a citizen of the country to buy land there.

speaker: ER
words: So I couldn't buy it.

speaker: SS
words: How big was the homeplace?

speaker: ER
words: Oh just a small place. But a lot of pasture land and wasteland in

speaker: ER
words: them mountains around there, but cultivated land is just

speaker: ER
words: if they had there Around, oh eight, ten acres cleared about all And then

speaker: ER
words: they had milk That's how they used to make living off of the farms

speaker: ER
words: back there they would grow hay ad a few oats and vegetables grow

speaker: ER
words: there

speaker: ER
words: good there,like potatoes and carrots. All kinds of vegetables grow real good.

speaker: ER
words: And then hay  the cows and then sold milk.

speaker: SS
words: Where did they grow the hay with eight acres of

speaker: ER
words: Well, they growed enough there to feed, oh—six or eight cows, somethin like that,

speaker: ER
words: Of course they cut some of thajwild hay there too in the old days. They'd go

speaker: ER
words: up on the slopes, you know, t on the mountains and cut wild hay,

speaker: ER
words: grass, put it un That was good cheep feed. Everybody had a few sheep them days

speaker: ER
words: too. And had goats too when I left there, quite a few goats.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds pretty much like the old days around here—the early days.

speaker: ER
words: Yeah. Similar, I guess, in the beginning here. We used to cut the wool

speaker: ER
words: off of the sheep there and they'd spin it and make cloth out of it, make

speaker: ER
words: thread and knitted socks and stuff, you know, out of the wool off the sheep.

speaker: ER
words: And the same thing we'd butcher maybe a cow, or a younger animal, yearling.

speaker: ER
words: And we'd take the hide and tan that and make shoes out of it,

speaker: ER
words: there

speaker: ER
words: leather. We got the bark off of the trees, off of birch or ash, you know. And

speaker: ER
words: we usti that bark for tanning the leather. Made our own shoes; I didn't have

speaker: ER
words: any boughten shoes until I come over to this country, made em at home.

speaker: SS
words: Did it take a long time to tan leather and make shoes out of it?

speaker: ER
words: Not very long. Well, fiat they let the hide dry, kind of. Then took it

speaker: ER
words: out to an old kind of a pond they had there and put the hide in the pond.

speaker: ER
words: And you let it lay there till the hair slips. It actually kind of starts

speaker: ER
words: to decay, you know, and the hair'll slip. And you get all the hair off of it.

speaker: ER
words: Then you put it in a wax, you know and put bark in there—you grind the bark.

speaker: ER
words: And then put it in there—I don't know how long they ha the hide in there.

speaker: ER
words: Oh, probably a month or two.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a fit did you get out of a pair of shoes like that?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, good, real good. We had good shoes. Yaah, they fitted good. We'd measure

speaker: ER
words: the foot and make the last, you know. We just did the whole thing. So it

speaker: ER
words: had like a regular sole on it?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, yes, yeah. We used wooden pegs, you

speaker: ER
words: know for fastening the soles oniinstead of sewing em on. Had them pegs about

speaker: ER
words: that long..Throw them in, then you took a rasp on the inside and th 'made

speaker: ER
words: rough so they wouldn't slip out. They were good, tight shoes. They were water

speaker: ER
words: proof. That leather was real good—it really held the water out. Of course,

speaker: ER
words: we had to grease it a lot, you know. Get the fat into it. Used grease off of

speaker: ER
words: y'know. We butchered a hog, and then we rubbed that grease into it.

speaker: ER
words: Got it soft.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a house did you have there?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, we had apretty good house. Yeah it was a It was a pretty big house.

speaker: ER
words: We had abig living room and upstairs. And then we had another

speaker: ER
words: living room that we didn't live in. You know in them days they had kind of

speaker: ER
words: special room but they didn't live in it. And then got company or

speaker: ER
words: somebody stayin overnight or something then they'd put them in that room

speaker: ER
words: there, you know. That was for company. Kept it nice all the time, you know.

speaker: ER
words: Kids couldn't even go in there (chackles)I got a picture of the house here.

speaker: ER
words: (Pause) Yeah. That's my niece's daughter there, getting married. It's the

speaker: ER
words: wedding.

speaker: SS
words: So it's right up against.

speaker: ER
words: Right up against the hill that's covered with mostly birch. Some ash in

speaker: ER
words: there-mountain ash-but nearly all birch. It's nearly all birch in that

speaker: ER
words: neighborhood, like^Rarasdale there. There wasn't hardly any pine or anything.

speaker: ER
words: But way back sometimes there'd bec a lot f big pines there. Because

speaker: ER
words: found 'em way down in the ground-big logs-that big, thick. We used to dig

speaker: ER
words: meadow big ditches that would drain some of them swamps, you know, i swamps there.

speaker: ER
words: And we'd dig down maybe eight or ten feet, them ditches,and we'd run into

speaker: ER
words: them pine logs. yellow pine logs. And there wasn't one of them growin

speaker: ER
words: there now. Not aone. So there really achange there some time or other.

speaker: SS
words: Did you use the birth wood?

speaker: ER
words: Yes, we used that for firewood. And we used the bark for tanning leather.

speaker: ER
words: But you know birch's got two layers, you know—one "outside layer, that

speaker: ER
words: thh stuff there, and then there's a thicker bark under there. Well, that

speaker: ER
words: outside layer, we used to make roofs out of that. That's the only kind of

speaker: ER
words: aroof we had when Iwas akid. We'd take that off in the spring when it would

speaker: ER
words: slip, you know, when the tree started growin. Take that off and we'd put it

speaker: ER
words: in layers and lay it out flat like this. And then we put rocks on there

speaker: ER
words: to keep it flat in big piles. And that's what we used for roof (In the barn,

speaker: ER
words: in the house and everything. We'd lay that there and then we'd

speaker: ER
words: slices of sod on top of that to hold it down so the wind wouldn't blow it

speaker: ER
words: off. We had that on the house too. We bad that on this house here but later

speaker: ER
words: they took it off and put that roofing on there now.

speaker: SS
words: Did it last for along time?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, them roofs last a long time; oh, twenty, tHrty years. Yeah, they were

speaker: ER
words: good roofs. Yep.

speaker: SS
words: What about your water supply?

speaker: ER
words: We had a little spring out this way here that we got our water. You see

speaker: ER
words: that big rocks that that basement?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: ER
words: I remember when they

speaker: ER
words: built this house; I was just a little kid then. I remember the old house

speaker: ER
words: we had. I don't know how old I was, about five probably—five years old when

speaker: ER
words: this house was built.

speaker: SS
words: Where did the lumber for it come from?

speaker: ER
words: Well, down closer to the ocean there they had lots of pine forests, y'know.

speaker: ER
words: And that's where we got logs for the lumber. And this house here ot the

speaker: ER
words: other house, the way they built them days they didn't have two by fours, you

speaker: ER
words: know, like they have nowadays. And there'd be a log about four inches thick,

speaker: ER
words: you know, and they just pur one on top of the other and they cut off the side

speaker: ER
words: of it and make boards out of it. So it was built out of four inch thick planks,

speaker: ER
words: you might say. That's how they built 'era. And then they put siding on the

speaker: ER
words: outside, boards.

speaker: SS
words: When you were a little boy there,what did kids do in Norway when they were

speaker: SS
words: growing up? Did they start school early?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, we were startin seven years old. And we had school in homes then. We

speaker: ER
words: didn't have schoolhouse. And I got all that there, they had a school in private

speaker: ER
words: homes. And hve the school in the same home all the time; they

speaker: ER
words: would rotate it, y'know. Then they'd have it down a little ways in the

speaker: ER
words: valley there one year and next year maybe up fartier, you know. There wasn't

speaker: ER
words: many kids there. I don't remember just how many we had to school there—

speaker: ER
words: maybe thirteen, fourteen kids when started school there. But then later

speaker: ER
words: on we built a new schoolhouse. So we all went to school there. But we only

speaker: ER
words: had terms of school. We'd have two weeks%there in that schoolhouse. And then

speaker: ER
words: farther down

speaker: ER
words: the same teacher, he'd go down,in the valley and have his school two weeks

speaker: ER
words: down there, see. So we had two weeks and then two weeks between each term.

speaker: SS
words: That doesn't sound too bad.

speaker: ER
words: No. (chuckles) But we had to do an awful lot of studying at home there. They

speaker: ER
words: gave us our lessons and we had to learn that at home by heart, y'know. When

speaker: ER
words: we came to school the next morning we had to know everything by heart. But

speaker: ER
words: in the first hour we generally had Bible history. We had a lesson in that

speaker: ER
words: that we had to know by heart. And then we had catechism.^ we had arithmetic

speaker: ER
words: and history, and then we had two hours a week singing practice. And then we

speaker: ER
words: I don't know

speaker: ER
words: had what they call that, you know, about animals and nature and all that,

speaker: ER
words: all that everyday. So we had pretty good school, leally, yeah. It's one thing

speaker: ER
words: there school, we couldn't get by with anything. We really had to

speaker: ER
words: study and know our lessons.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have a lot of books in this school? Books for the subjects or was it

speaker: SS
words: mostly just.

speaker: ER
words: Each one of us had a book and we took the book home with us. We had our own

speaker: ER
words: book. Learned our lessons at home.

speaker: SS
words: It was all the kids in one room just like the old schoolhouses here.

speaker: ER
words: All the classes in one room.

speaker: SS
words: What about play?

speaker: ER
words: Oh yeah, we had sports. We didn't have basketball or football or anything

speaker: ER
words: like that but we had foot races and high jump and long jump and a lot of

speaker: ER
words: games, you know, we played in school. And we had tree planting every spring.

speaker: ER
words: The whole school got out for a couple of days and planted trees. That was

speaker: ER
words: quite interesting. Some of the trees that I helped plant when I was a kid in

speaker: ER
words: school, they harvesting WhoU back tnere* And that was klnd of Interesting.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have anyhorses on the farm?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, we had horses. one or two, sometimes we had a brood mare and that

speaker: ER
words: a colt each year. We had horse and buggy; we used single horse to drive

speaker: ER
words: that, din't use two horses like they did here.While some of em used single

speaker: ER
words: here too, y'know. But we only had one horse and a small buggy that we used

speaker: ER
words: when we went to town or to church wherever we went.

speaker: SS
words: When you were at home and not at school were you expected to work most of

speaker: SS
words: the time or could you play most of the time?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, we did a lot of playing. Oh course, we had to work too when there was

speaker: ER
words: work to do. We uged up to them steep mountains

speaker: ER
words: and  down by hand  quite aways

speaker: ER
words: while gettin the firewood

speaker: ER
words: until  with ahorse to haul it. And then in the summertime we'd cut that

speaker: ER
words: wild hay in the mountains there. We packed it down, you know.

speaker: ER
words: We had a little building that we put it in, that most of our hay was pacled in.

speaker: ER
words: rough, you couldn't have it with a horse. So, we had our chores

speaker: ER
words: to do all right. But then we played a lot too, you know. Kids get together

speaker: ER
words: and then

speaker: ER
words: and play and have games. had young people's organizations, like I know

speaker: ER
words: we had a temperance organization that I belonged to. And we had meetings

speaker: ER
words: and we had plays there sometimes, programs. So we had a pretty good time,

speaker: ER
words: really. Well, they had ,here too when I came here fiat. We had a lot of

speaker: ER
words: home entertainments here like we had parties in the wintertime; we used to

speaker: ER
words: go from house to house and have parties. And we had literary. We had that in

speaker: ER
words: the schoolhouse. And we had plays there and a aat of things goin on.

speaker: ER
words: And then the Church down here. It was kind of a center for this

speaker: ER
words: community here, y'know. And we had choir down there; choir practice about

speaker: ER
words: twice a week. We had singing, and in the summertime we had picnics and

speaker: ER
words: me  remember. We turned that old ice cream freezer a lot.

speaker: ER
words: And that ice cream, y'know.(Chuckles) That was really good, y'know that

speaker: ER
words: homemade ice cream. Used to put ice and salt in there. Used to put up

speaker: ER
words: ice here in the wintertime.

speaker: SS
words: Here?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, yeah. We cut them square off of the ponds or wherever you have water that

speaker: ER
words: froze. You take and saw that ice into square blocks and then put it in the

speaker: ER
words: building and put sawdust all around it. And that ice would keep there pretty

speaker: ER
words: near all next summer if you put enough sawdust around it. It'd be ice there

speaker: ER
words: all summer.

speaker: SS
words: I didn know that they did that here.

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, we did that here. And that's the ice they used to use in ice cream.

speaker: SS
words: Would you do that in a group? Getting the ice?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, most of the time a few of them went together. They used to work together

speaker: ER
words: quite a bit, you know, in the old days here. Like butchering, you know. Most

speaker: ER
words: of them would butcher in the fall. They'd butcher hogs. Most families butchered

speaker: ER
words: four or five hogs. And then they'd get together, see. Butcher hogs, and they'd

speaker: ER
words: do it all up in one day. Cut 'em up and salt *em down.a Sugar cure 'em and

speaker: ER
words: then rend the lard and make sausage and all ttft. Do it in one day, the whole

speaker: ER
words: thing.

speaker: ER
words: The same thing about thrashing here. They helped one another, thrashing

speaker: ER
words: grain. Exchange work.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it was a lot like here the way it was when you grew up as far

speaker: SS
words: as cooperating?

speaker: ER
words: Quite a bit. Yeah, when I first came here it was quite a bit like the old

speaker: ER
words: country. Maybe a little farther advanced here than back there. It seined like

speaker: ER
words: here, this country's always kind of been aiead of the rest of the countries.

speaker: ER
words: And the rest of them, they'll finally. Like the automobile, you know,

speaker: ER
words: I think it first came here and then finally they got 'em everywhere now.

speaker: SS
words: But it seems to me that there was such a small amount of land that you had

speaker: SS
words: there. It'd be hard to get ahead in yoar country.

speaker: ER
words: Well, it was hard to get ahead, just made a livin—that's about all

speaker: ER
words: Pretty hard to get ahead much. But it seemed like they had a pretty good living

speaker: ER
words: back there. They always had plenty to eat and we had clothes, yoy know, and

speaker: ER
words: everything. We got along all right, but didn't have anymoney hardly. Money

speaker: ER
words: was really scarce.

speaker: SS
words: What was the town of  like?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, it's just a small shopping place there. They had two stores, I think, where

speaker: ER
words: you could buy a few things. They didn't have many groceries in the stores

speaker: ER
words: some

speaker: ER
words: them days, only  flour and sugar, coffee and salt and pepper, a few spices

speaker: ER
words: and oh, a few more items. But they didn't have many varieties in the grocery

speaker: ER
words: stores, them days like they have now. And they had a chush there. That's where

speaker: ER
words: everybody in that neighborhood went to church. There was only one church in

speaker: ER
words: the whole area there, you know, Lutheran church. And the steamers come in there.

speaker: ER
words: Brought the mail in there, and crates, whatever you needed and people, when

speaker: ER
words: they traveled they went by the steamers. You went into a big town, you went

speaker: ER
words: into  and you got on the boat and went to bigger cities. And

speaker: ER
words: they had a school there, too. Pretty good sized schoolhouse there in

speaker: ER
words: And the graveyard--it was there in . And it was just a small place

speaker: ER
words: there. It's a little bit bigger now than then. They built a new school there.

speaker: ER
words: Now the whole valley goes down there to school. They followed the same as they

speaker: ER
words: have here. They consolidated and they have bus now. They all go down there to^

speaker: SS
words: What was the herring run that you mentioned to me before?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, yeah. That was in the spring of the year. . .(Break)

speaker: ER
words: that's in Norwegian, you know, that's spring. And the spring herring

speaker: ER
words: run, it would start in the last January February and March. And everybody

speaker: ER
words: used to try to go out on that to make a little money, you know. I went out

speaker: ER
words: two seasons. I was just a little kid, I was about fifteen years old, I guess,

speaker: ER
words: the first time I went out. And those schools, y'know, herring,they come

speaker: ER
words: in to spawn in on the sandbanks on the shore there. And there was alot of

speaker: ER
words: other fish, too, y'know, lived on the herring. Like whale, you cold see the

speaker: ER
words: whale cH: there to, you know. But they wouldn't come in very close. But they

speaker: ER
words: were out there, could see em. And then the

speaker: ER
words: herring come in. Why, we'd surround them with a  y'know, so they couldn't

speaker: ER
words: get away. crew --a crew of about twelve, I think. Each crew was

speaker: ER
words: lust about twelve man. And we lived on the boat. It

speaker: ER
words: was a sailboat. And the boss, he'd be up on the hill some nights, you know,

speaker: ER
words: watching for these herring schools. 6n a clear night you can see the

speaker: ER
words: reflection on the sky, you know, from the herring. They come up fairly close

speaker: ER
words: to the surface of the water and it reflects on the sky. Something like the

speaker: ER
words: northern lights, you know. And he'd watch that reflection where they went,

speaker: ER
words: and then if they got in close to land some place h thought he cold get

speaker: ER
words: 'em surrounded, why he'd hurry down and get everybody out of bed and out

speaker: ER
words: we'd go with that net and surround them. And then we had them. They couldn*t

speaker: ER
words: get away.

speaker: SS
words: How'd you get the nets around them? Did you wade in the water or did use

speaker: SS
words: the boats?

speaker: ER
words: No, rowboats. See the nets, you know, they have rocks in the bottom and

speaker: ER
words: cork ot^the top. So you throw 'era out, they make awall, y'know—one end

speaker: ER
words: goes down to the bottom and corks on the top holds it floatin so *won't

speaker: ER
words: sink. And then they'd get in touch with some of the buyers. the

speaker: ER
words: times I was out there we sold the herring to Englishmen. They come in with

speaker: ER
words: the steamers there from England and we'd load »em up with herring. They off;

speaker: ER
words: they put the herring in boxes. They had ice with em. So they used shovels.

speaker: ER
words: They'd shovel the herring in and then a shovel of ice they filled the box

speaker: ER
words: up. Some of them steamers h about two thousand boxes of herring. And a box

speaker: ER
words: would hold just about a barrel, I guess. But then we worked and day

speaker: ER
words: till we got the boat loaded. everybody got together then, too, you know.

speaker: ER
words: the boat come in they wanted to get loaded just as soon as possible

speaker: ER
words: on account of the icewould melt, I suppose. So you had to everybody get dfc

speaker: ER
words: and workA get the boat loaded. So that was quite a business them days.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a catch would you come up with in one run in a rowboat?With

speaker: SS
words: your net out?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, I think we had some catches there up to, oh, I'd say two or three

speaker: ER
words: thousand barrels, you know. Oh yeah, it was big schools come in there. Lots

speaker: ER
words: of herring. And once in a while we'd cath some cod, too, or other fish in

speaker: ER
words: there with . One time out there, I see the herring and the fish come in,

speaker: ER
words: and they were so tight, they come up kinda on the shore there, the waves

speaker: ER
words: come in you know and then the herring come in. And then the wave go back

speaker: ER
words: out again and lay there and kick in the sand there

speaker: ER
words: That's how they were crowded up against the land there. And we went out in

speaker: ER
words: the rowboat there with hooks, y'know. And we'd hook a lot of codfish

speaker: ER
words: right out of the rowboat. They were right up on the surface, so tight in

speaker: ER
words: there, you know. Just packed in there. We'd catch 'em just with a hook.

speaker: SS
words: Well, how long did this run last?

speaker: ER
words: Oh, ' used to ; around a month and a half, two months. The way

speaker: ER
words: they actually worked the they started up in the northern part there

speaker: ER
words: earlier. then later on they seemed like they moved south, and we'd

speaker: ER
words: move with 'em, you know. We'd go down south and maybe we'd catch another

speaker: ER
words: school down there. So we jsedto be out there for a month and a half, two

speaker: ER
words: months.

speaker: SS
words: And you'd leave to go further south?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, on a sailboat; we all lived on a sailboat. I'll tell you what kind

speaker: ER
words: of grub we had with us, we took a grub stake with us. We'd buy a whole barrel

speaker: ER
words: full of  you know, or we call 'em  That's a dry toast

speaker: ER
words: first winter, yep. Yeah, I'm pretty sure it was the first winter.

speaker: ER
words: Well, I worked for em off and on, not steady since I atarted farmin. I started

words: kinda made that my headquarters there, you know. But I worked for neighbors

speaker: ER
words: around there. But Nick had a lot of livestock, you know: horses and cattle.

words: So in the wintertime there we had to feed all them horses and cattle, and

words: haul maure. I remember one winter I hauled manure there, I don't know, over

words: horses. We had a lot of piea that piled up, you know. Trying to get it ail

words: Just into Tsoy?

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, um hum. Sacks, you know. Everything was sacked them days. When they

words: like that,you know. You could get way up high, pile it up.

speaker: ER
words: then

speaker: SS
words: for other people.

speaker: ER
words: Oh, the first land I rented was tat forty down there where Lester

words: lives. And I growed beans then; I put it all in beans. And that's the

speaker: ER
words: we growed a lot of beans days, we didn't grow any peas. It was all

speaker: ER
words: beans and wheat and oats and barley. We generally had some of our land in

speaker: ER
words: beans and then they put the bean ground into fall wheat in the fall. That

speaker: ER
words: was the notation they had here then instead of now they have peas and then

speaker: ER
words: put the fall wheat on the pea ground or go the

speaker: ER
words: first crop I growed was beans—white beans, white navy beans. And we planted

speaker: ER
words: them in rows and cultivated them. Then we cut em with a bean cutter with two

speaker: ER
words: knives. It kinda went a little bit under the ground, cut the beans off, and

speaker: ER
words: they come out in a row behind. Them knives, about this much space behind and

speaker: ER
words: then the knives come in and the beans would kind of slide back on the knife

speaker: ER
words: and drc%ff the end back there. Then we'd shock *em with a pitchfork, put

speaker: ER

speaker: ER
words: in the summer and kept it clean from weeds, and then you held the moisture too,

speaker: ER
words: by cultivating. So the wheat done real good on the bean ground.

speaker: ER

speaker: ER

speaker: ER

speaker: ER
words: place where they worked and they got their board, room.

words: then you'd go out and work for other people in season.

speaker: ER
words: Yeah, sometimes I 'd kinda come back there, and I stayed there. One year I

words: there and mauled  But that was after I started farmin, though.

words: Like in harvest. And that was the main income of a lot the working

words: good run in harvest and they'd save up enough money to carry 'era over maybe.
